### The Central Trust Bank

Recommendation: pursue
Scores: ICP 94 | Zurich appetite 73 | Pursuit priority 68 | Evidence confidence 84
Website: https://www.centralbank.net
Subsegment / SIC: Regional state-chartered commercial bank within Central Bancompany, with retail/commercial banking, mortgage, wealth/trust, HSA and payments/technology operations / 6022 State Banks, Federal Reserve

Why this account:
Keep as pursue, but with a narrower meaning: this is strong enough for Zurich sales/distribution to validate internally and with broker intelligence now, not for immediate quote activity. The account is clearly in Zurich FI scope, has scale for multiline need, and has concrete public-company, leadership, technology, branch-expansion and financial-lines/cyber triggers. The main calibration issue is that broker, renewal, incumbent carrier and Zurich-history evidence is absent, and property appetite is conditional on CAT/COPE details across a large multi-state branch footprint.

Why now:
- Central Bancompany's public-company transition and Nasdaq ticker create a timely D&O/EPL/fiduciary/cyber and total-cost-of-risk review trigger.
- The June 9, 2025 CFO appointment is a usable public timing hook for broker, carrier panel and insurance-program validation.
- 2025 results cite profitability, deposits, IPO proceeds, technology investment and capital deployment, supporting a current risk-financing conversation.
- SEC filing statements about planned branches in St. Louis, Kansas City Metro and Denver create property, construction/vendor-risk and branch-operations triggers.
- Mortgage, wealth/trust, HSA and payments operations broaden the risk discussion beyond a simple community-bank package.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Financial Lines, Cyber, Property, General Liability, Workers Compensation, Umbrella, Claims/Risk Engineering, Auto

Positive evidence:
- FDIC records show The Central Trust Bank is active, headquartered in Jefferson City, Missouri, with reported assets, deposits and a large office footprint as of March 31, 2026. Implication: Confirms in-scope bank status, scale and a meaningful premises/property schedule.
- FDIC fields show state charter and Federal Reserve regulator, supporting SIC 6022 State Banks, Federal Reserve. Implication: Zurich should evaluate this through the Financial Institutions appetite, not as a generic services account.
- Central Bancompany describes a multi-state banking organization with banking, investment advisory, trust, mortgage, HSA, technology and online operations. Implication: Supports cross-sell angles across property, casualty, cyber, fiduciary/trust, mortgage and payments exposures.
- SEC reporting describes full-service branches, thousands of employees, consumer banking, commercial banking, mortgage, wealth/trust and brokerage operations. Implication: Supports multiline Property, GL, WC, Umbrella, Financial Lines and Cyber discussion with a sophisticated buyer set.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- No public broker, incumbent carrier, renewal date or Zurich relationship evidence is provided. Implication: This is the largest pursuit-quality gap; Zurich should validate relationship ownership and broker path before any external sales motion.
- The footprint includes Midwest and selected higher-CAT geographies, creating tornado, hail, wind, flood and hurricane review needs. Implication: Property appetite is conditional on location schedule, modeled CAT, COPE, flood zones and mitigation.
- SEC 10-K real-estate loan concentration and CRE/construction exposure create foreclosure, OREO, environmental and collateral-management questions. Implication: Underwriting should not assume limited foreclosed-property exposure without schedules and controls.
- Large branch and ATM/customer-facing premises footprint.
- Midwest tornado/hail exposure plus Colorado and Florida/Naples wind, flood and hurricane exposure.
- Cyber, online/mobile banking, payments, HSA and third-party technology dependency.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Current broker, package carrier, Financial Lines tower, Cyber carrier, renewal dates, retentions and Zurich account history are not public. Implication: Critical first validation step for distribution strategy and white-space assessment.
- Branch-level COPE, construction, sprinklers, alarms, cameras, vault/ATM controls, water mitigation and maintenance practices are not public. Implication: Property and GL appetite cannot be finalized without underwriting data.
- Fleet, hired/non-owned auto, cash transport, courier, mobile banking and repossession exposures were not publicly verified. Implication: Auto should remain a conditional line until exposures are quantified.
- Formal disaster recovery and business continuity documentation was not found in the profile evidence. Implication: Ask for BCP/DR testing, incident response, backups and critical vendor recovery evidence.

  - Outreach angle: Frame outreach as a short validation conversation for a new public-company CFO responsible for finance, treasury and procurement: Zurich can help benchmark financial-lines, cyber and multiline total cost of risk after the IPO transition, while pressure-testing broker path, retentions, branch/CAT property data needs and technology/vendor-risk controls before any quote discussion.

Open underwriting questions:
- Is Zurich already incumbent, previously quoted, declined, blocked by broker relationship or relationship-owned?

### The Park National Bank

Recommendation: pursue
Scores: ICP 94 | Zurich appetite 72 | Pursuit priority 68 | Evidence confidence 78
Website: https://www.parknationalbank.com
Subsegment / SIC: Regional/community commercial bank with multi-state branch banking, business banking, wealth/investment-adjacent services, and bank-holding-company parentage / 6021 National Commercial Banks

Why this account:
Park National Bank remains an in-scope regional bank with credible multiline FI insurance need and a concrete 2026 merger/leadership-change trigger. The account is suitable for active Zurich internal and broker/distribution validation now, especially around post-merger property schedule consolidation, CAT screening, cyber, financial lines, casualty, WC, umbrella, and claims/risk engineering. Scores are modestly downgraded because public evidence does not identify the incumbent broker/carriers, renewal dates, Zurich white space, branch-level COPE/security controls, or post-merger program structure.

Why now:
- Park completed its First Citizens National Bank merger on February 1, 2026, expanding into Tennessee and creating a near-term policy, property schedule, cyber, vendor, D&O/E&O, and claims-integration review moment.
- Matthew R. Miller became CEO of Park National Corporation and Park National Bank effective January 1, 2026, while David L. Trautman remained board chair, creating a new executive-risk-governance window.
- The combined company publicly reports more than 100 branches across Ohio, Kentucky, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Tennessee, so CAT, premises, security, and workers compensation exposures likely changed after the merger.
- First Citizens customers were told conversion activities would occur later in 2026, making integration risk and program harmonization timely sales-distribution validation topics.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Property, General Liability, Workers Compensation, Umbrella, Financial Lines, Cyber, Claims/Risk Engineering, Auto

Positive evidence:
- Park National Bank reports 112 individual locations, about $13.0 billion in total assets, 1,950 associates, and more than 100 branches plus 150+ ATMs in five states. Implication: Large enough for a meaningful multiline middle-market FI program, with credible property, GL, WC, umbrella, cyber, financial-lines, and claims/risk-engineering entry points.
- The February 1, 2026 First Citizens merger extended Park into Tennessee and created a combined company with pro forma total assets of $12.6 billion, deposits of $10.5 billion, loans of $9.6 billion, and more than 100 branches. Implication: M&A integration is a strong why-now trigger for Zurich to validate broker ownership, harmonize property schedules, review deductibles/limits, and pitch cyber/financial-lines and claims analytics.
- Park announced Matthew R. Miller as CEO of both Park National Corporation and Park National Bank effective January 1, 2026, with David L. Trautman continuing as board chair. Implication: CEO transition supports outreach framed around total cost of risk, post-merger governance, risk controls, and broker/carrier program benchmarking.
- Public 2025 results show other real estate owned of $729,000 at December 31, 2025, small relative to the bank's balance sheet. Implication: Visible OREO appears limited, which is favorable versus bank programs with large foreclosed-property schedules, though Zurich should verify post-merger OREO and repossessed collateral.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- The branch footprint now spans Ohio, Kentucky, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Tennessee, which introduces CAT screening needs for wind, hail, flood, convective storm, and regional crime variations. Implication: Do not quote appetite off corporate averages; geocode all branches and ATMs before property/CAT positioning.
- Public location examples show branches with ATMs, drive-thru service, and night-drop service. Implication: Premises security, ATM servicing, cash handling, robbery controls, alarms, cameras, and any guard usage need underwriting validation.
- The First Citizens transaction includes customer conversion activities later in 2026, which can elevate operational, vendor, cyber, E&O, and change-management risk during integration. Implication: Frame outreach around integration risk controls and policy harmonization; avoid assuming loss-free integration.
- Multi-state branch property schedule with Southeastern exposure requiring CAT, flood, wind, hail, crime, and water-damage screening.
- Retail bank cash, ATM, drive-thru, and night-drop exposure with public security-control details unavailable.
- 2026 merger and conversion activity may increase operational, cyber, vendor, E&O, and D&O change-management exposures.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Current insurance broker, incumbent carriers, Zurich prior submissions, relationship owner, and renewal dates were not visible in the submitted public evidence. Implication: Pursuit should begin with Zurich CRM/submission/loss-history and broker-channel validation before direct outreach.
- Branch-level COPE data, sprinkler status, monitored alarms, cameras, water mitigation, crime scores, and flood/wind/hail zones are not public at underwriting depth. Implication: Required before appetite can move from sales validation to underwriting indication.
- Fleet, courier, mobile-branch, cash-transport, hired/non-owned auto, repossession, and armed-guard exposures are not publicly confirmed. Implication: Auto and GL/security appetite depends on whether these are outsourced with strong risk transfer or handled internally.
- Post-merger legacy First Citizens insurance program integration, deductibles, limits, retentions, and claims history are unknown. Implication: A broker meeting should test whether the merger creates a near-term consolidation or midterm endorsement opportunity.

- Run Zurich internal account lookup for Park National Bank, Park National Corporation, First Citizens Bancshares, and First Citizens National Bank before outreach.

- How are First Citizens legacy policies, claims, retentions, and property schedules being integrated during 2026 conversion?
- Are cash transport, ATM servicing, guards, couriers, repossessions, foreclosed-property maintenance, and facilities vendors outsourced with indemnity, COI, waiver-of-subrogation, and minimum-limit requirements?
- What are the bank's BCP/DR testing cadence, cyber incident history, MFA/EDR/vendor controls, and ransomware response protocols?

### First Commonwealth Bank

Recommendation: pursue
Scores: ICP 93 | Zurich appetite 72 | Pursuit priority 67 | Evidence confidence 82
Website: https://www.fcbanking.com
Subsegment / SIC: Regional commercial bank and bank holding-company subsidiary with commercial banking, mortgage, equipment finance, wealth management, and affiliated insurance-agency operations / 6029 Commercial Banks, NEC

Why this account:
Approve as a calibrated pursue, but only for Zurich sales/distribution validation before any underwriting commitment. First Commonwealth is a strong in-scope regional-bank account with public evidence of scale, branch/property footprint, regulated FI operations, cyber governance, and recent CenterBank integration that creates a credible why-now trigger. Scores are reduced because the profile lacks public broker, renewal, incumbent-carrier, Zurich relationship, loss-history, COPE, crime/security, fleet, and OREO evidence; those gaps must be resolved before quote strategy or appetite commitment.

Why now:
- CenterBank/CenterGroup integration and June 2025 conversion/rebranding provide a concrete reason to validate updated property schedules, systems integration, cyber controls, BCP/DR, vendor contracts, and insurance program structure.
- The company reports a 126-office PA/OH footprint, supporting multiline property, GL, WC, umbrella, cyber, financial-lines, and claims/risk-engineering discovery.
- Assets above $10B create regulatory and operational-resilience discussion angles, including CFPB/FDIC scrutiny disclosed in the annual-report evidence.
- Public 2026 operating activity cited in the profile may help buyer-access planning, but the review treats it as a weaker trigger unless tied to specific press-release URLs in follow-on buyer research.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Property, General Liability, Workers Compensation, Umbrella, Financial Lines, Cyber, Claims/Risk Engineering, Auto

Positive evidence:
- First Commonwealth Financial Corporation is headquartered in Indiana, Pennsylvania and operates a regional banking footprint across Pennsylvania and Ohio, including 126 community banking offices and related commercial lending and mortgage operations. Implication: Strong regional-bank ICP fit with enough branch/property footprint for a multiline Zurich validation conversation.
- At December 31, 2025, First Commonwealth reported approximately $12.3B in assets, $9.8B in loans, $10.3B in deposits, and $1.6B in shareholders' equity. Implication: Scale fits a middle-market/regional FI pursuit where Zurich risk engineering, claims analytics, cyber, and financial-lines capabilities may be relevant.
- The company offers commercial banking, consumer banking, mortgage, equipment finance, wealth management, and insurance products through First Commonwealth Bank and First Commonwealth Insurance Agency. Implication: Creates multiple underwriting and sales angles, including property/casualty, WC, umbrella, cyber, financial lines, bond/crime, and agency E&O diligence.
- The CenterBank/CenterGroup acquisition and subsequent conversion/rebranding added Cincinnati-market offices and operations, creating a plausible post-integration insurance review trigger. Implication: M&A integration gives Zurich a concrete reason to ask about updated schedules, valuations, controls, cyber integration, BCP, vendor risk transfer, and claims history.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- No public broker, incumbent carrier, renewal date, Zurich relationship, submission history, or white-space evidence is provided. Implication: This is the main quality-gate weakness; pursue should mean immediate broker/internal validation, not quote readiness.
- First Commonwealth owns an affiliated insurance agency and earns insurance commissions, which may complicate the corporate insurance placement or producer relationship path. Implication: Validate whether the agency is purely a customer-facing business or has influence over corporate risk placement.
- The annual-report evidence indicates a large owned/leased premises schedule, including owned and leased banking offices and a historically significant headquarters complex. Implication: Attractive property opportunity, but appetite is conditional on COPE, valuation, sprinkler, water mitigation, alarm, flood, wind/hail, and older-building controls.
- No public broker, renewal, incumbent-carrier, Zurich-status, or white-space evidence.
- Large owned/leased branch-property schedule across PA/OH requires COPE, valuation, water, flood, wind/hail, winter-freeze, and protection review.
- Historic headquarters complex may present older-building construction/protection questions.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Corporate insurance buyer, risk manager, broker of record, carrier panel, renewal month, Zurich incumbent status, prior quote/declination history, and loss history are not public. Implication: Run Zurich CRM, submission, claims, loss-history, and broker-channel checks before outreach.
- Branch-level COPE, sprinklers, monitored alarms, cameras, flood/wind/hail scores, winter-freeze controls, water-damage controls, and maintenance programs are not public. Implication: Require schedule-level property engineering triage before any property appetite commitment.
- Cash handling, ATM servicing, armed/unarmed guard use, robbery history, crime coverage, and branch security procedures are not public. Implication: Crime/security controls should be part of the first underwriting discovery request.
- Fleet size, hired/non-owned auto exposure, employee mileage reimbursement, mobile banking, repossession practices, courier activity, and cash-transport arrangements are not public. Implication: Auto should remain secondary until controlled-fleet and HNOA exposure are confirmed.

Priority buyers and paths:
- James R. Reske, Executive Vice President, Chief Financial Officer and Treasurer (priority 1, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: Position Zurich as offering a post-CenterBank integration insurance-program validation for a CFO/Treasurer: updated property schedules and values, cyber and vendor integration, financial-lines retentions, branch security/crime controls, claims analytics, and premium-efficiency benchmarking before the next renewal, while making clear that any appetite discussion depends on broker, loss, COPE, and incumbent-program validation.
  - Business paths: Use First Commonwealth's public Contact Us page and Contact Us By Email path: https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/contact-us/; Use the public Message Our Leadership path on the company contact page for executive-routed business feedback; avoid account-specific or sensitive information.; Use the official investor-relations/officers biography as the verified executive profile path.
  - Warm paths: Georgetown University Law Center alumni network, based on verified JD education.; The Ohio State University alumni network, based on verified master's and combined bachelor's/master's degrees.; CFA Institute or regional CFA society network, based on verified CFA credential.
- Linda Metzmaier, Executive Vice President, Chief Risk Officer (priority 1, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Lead with a post-CenterBank integration risk review tailored to Metzmaier's expanded CRO remit: updated branch/property schedules, cyber-control integration, third-party/vendor risk, fraud and branch security, BCP/DR readiness, and FI financial-lines/crime loss scenarios. Position Zurich as helping validate controls and identify insurable gaps before renewal or market check, not as asking for quote data prematurely.
  - Business paths: Official First Commonwealth Contact Us / press-release route through corporate communications or executive-office referral: https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/press-releases/; Investor Relations Officers & Directors page for executive-role validation and corporate routing: https://firstcommonwealthbank.q4ir.com/corporate-information/officers-directors/default.aspx; LinkedIn professional profile path: https://www.linkedin.com/in/linda-metzmaier-6a3bb430
  - Warm paths: Bloomfield College alumni connection, if Zurich, a broker, or a client executive has a relevant alumni tie.; Former Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and bank compliance/audit network, based on her prior OCC and compliance leadership background disclosed in First Commonwealth's release.; Pennsylvania and Ohio regional banking risk/compliance ecosystem, especially CRO, CCO, information-security, third-party-risk, and BCP/DR forums; no specific membership was verified publicly.
- Matthew Tomb, Executive Vice President, Chief Legal Officer / General Counsel and Secretary (priority 2, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: Frame outreach around the post-CenterBank integration and the 2025 legal/risk realignment: offer Zurich's FI-focused view on risk-transfer terms, vendor indemnities, claims protocols, cyber/BCP alignment, D&O/E&O implications, and branch-property schedule validation before any underwriting commitment. Position it as a legal and risk-governance discussion, not a quote push.
  - Business paths: Company executive/contact route through First Commonwealth leadership/contact pages: https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/leadership/ and https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/contact-us/; Investor relations/corporate governance route through First Commonwealth Financial Corporation Officers & Directors page: https://firstcommonwealthbank.q4ir.com/corporate-information/officers-directors/default.aspx; LinkedIn profile path for business-context outreach: https://www.linkedin.com/in/matthew-tomb-bb236a36
  - Warm paths: Duke Law alumni network, especially financial-services, banking-regulatory, cyber, or D&O/E&O counsel connections.; University of Pennsylvania alumni network, particularly finance, insurance, legal, or regional-bank contacts in Pennsylvania and Ohio.; Central Ohio business and deal ecosystem contacts, given public LinkedIn location signals in the Dublin/Lewis Center, Ohio area and First Commonwealth's Ohio expansion.
- Carrie Riggle, Executive Vice President, Human Resources Manager (priority 3, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: Use a narrow HR-risk angle, not a generic insurance pitch: reference her public EVP HR role and banking talent-management involvement, then offer Zurich support for a post-CenterBank workforce and branch-footprint review focused on WC claim trends, EPL exposure, employee safety, workplace violence readiness, return-to-work practices, and training consistency across Pennsylvania and Ohio locations, while separately validating enterprise insurance authority with risk/finance and the broker.
  - Business paths: Official company route: use First Commonwealth's public leadership/contact paths, including https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/leadership/ and https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/contact-us/, rather than personal contact data.; LinkedIn business profile path: https://www.linkedin.com/in/carrie-riggle-72955732.; Association path: Consumer Bankers Association Talent Management Committee lists Riggle and provides a professional HR/talent-management context for peer or event-based outreach.
  - Warm paths: Pennsylvania Bankers Association relationship path: public evidence ties Riggle to PA Bankers board or policy-committee activity, including member engagement and development.; Consumer Bankers Association path: CBA Talent Management Committee membership creates a peer-network entry around HR, talent, employee experience, and regulatory/workforce change in banking.; Women in Banking path: PA Bankers materials list Riggle as a 2020 Champion for Women - Member Bank award recipient, suggesting a professional DEI/leadership-network angle.
- Kris Levan, Senior Vice President, Chief Marketing and Experience Officer (priority 3, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Frame outreach as a low-pressure post-CenterBank integration and branch-operations risk discussion: Zurich can help review branch customer/employee safety, premises loss drivers, service-continuity lessons, claims trends, and training implications across the PA/OH footprint, while asking Kris to advise on the right risk, finance, facilities, or legal owner for any insurance-program validation.
  - Business paths: Company leadership page: executive-team contact is routed through First Commonwealth contact channels, with media inquiries directed to Communications@fcbanking.com.; Company contact page path: https://www.fcbanking.com/why-us/contact-us/; LinkedIn profile path: https://www.linkedin.com/in/krislevan
  - Warm paths: Indiana University of Pennsylvania alumni network, based on verified education.; Indiana County Chamber of Commerce board and Marketing and Membership Committee network.; YMCA of Indiana County board and governance network.

Next actions:
- Keep as pursue for immediate Zurich distribution/internal validation, not automatic quoting.
- Run Zurich internal account, submission, claims, loss, and producer-of-record checks for First Commonwealth Bank, First Commonwealth Financial Corporation, and First Commonwealth Insurance Agency.
- Ask target brokers or Zurich distribution contacts for renewal timing, incumbent carriers, current program structure, and whether CenterBank integration created a market-check opportunity.
- Lead with a post-acquisition risk review covering updated property schedules, cyber integration, BCP/DR, branch security, claims analytics, and risk-engineering support.
- Proceed with public/business-safe buyer mapping for CFO/Treasurer, CRO, COO, General Counsel, Facilities/Real Estate, HR, and insurance/procurement stakeholders.

Open underwriting questions:
- Is Zurich currently on the account, has Zurich quoted or declined it, and who owns the producer relationship?
- What is the renewal date and current carrier structure for property, GL, WC, auto, umbrella, cyber, bond/crime, D&O/E&O, EPL, fiduciary, and professional liability?
- Provide the complete branch/property schedule with COPE, valuations, protection, alarms/cameras, sprinklers, flood zones, and recent losses.
- How were CenterBank locations and systems integrated into insurance schedules, cyber controls, BCP/DR, vendor contracts, and incident response?
- What are the bank's cash-handling, ATM servicing, guard, robbery-prevention, and crime-loss controls?
- What fleet, hired/non-owned auto, employee mileage, courier, cash transport, repossession, and mobile-banking exposures exist?

### Dime Commercial Bank

Recommendation: pursue
Scores: ICP 92 | Zurich appetite 70 | Pursuit priority 66 | Evidence confidence 82
Website: https://www.dime.com
Subsegment / SIC: Regional commercial bank / New York State-chartered commercial bank serving New York metro, Long Island, Westchester, Staten Island and Lakewood, NJ, with title insurance and financial-planning/investment services subsidiaries. / 6022 State Banks, Federal Reserve

Why this account:
Keep as a cautious pursue for Zurich sales/distribution validation, not as a quote-ready account. Dime is clearly inside the FI ICP as a roughly $15 billion state-chartered commercial bank with 62 branches and regulated banking operations. The June 2026 rebrand/name change is a concrete why-now trigger, and the branch footprint supports multiline Property/GL/WC/Umbrella plus Financial Lines/Cyber discussion. Scores are trimmed because underwriting appetite is conditional on NY metro/Long Island property aggregation, ATM/cash-handling controls, CRE/multifamily credit concentration, and because public sources do not identify broker, incumbent carriers, renewal timing, or Zurich white space.

Why now:
- Name change and rebrand to Dime Commercial Bank became effective June 2, 2026, with NYSE opening-bell visibility on June 2, 2026.
- The company reports approximately $15 billion in assets and number-one deposit market share among sub-$20B commercial banks on Greater Long Island, supporting middle-market FI relevance rather than Fortune 500-only handling.
- Dime opened a full-service Madison Avenue branch in Manhattan in September 2025 and disclosed 62 branch locations as of March 31, 2026, giving Zurich a current property/branch-operations entry point.
- Recent public filings show credit-quality and CRE/multifamily concentration disclosures, creating a natural Financial Lines, cyber, claims analytics, and risk-governance discussion.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Property, General Liability, Workers Compensation, Umbrella, Financial Lines, Cyber, Claims/Risk Engineering

Positive evidence:
- Dime Commercial Bancshares is a bank holding company engaged in commercial banking and financial services through wholly owned Dime Commercial Bank; the bank is headquartered in Hauppauge and has over 60 locations spanning Montauk to Manhattan, Staten Island, Westchester and Lakewood, NJ. Implication: Strong ICP fit and enough physical footprint for a multiline Property/GL/WC/Umbrella and claims/risk-engineering conversation.
- The June 1, 2026 release says the Dime Commercial Bank name change became effective June 2, 2026 and describes the bank as a New York State-chartered trust company with approximately $15 billion in assets. Implication: Fresh rebrand is a timely reason to validate broker, incumbent carriers, renewal timing, and whether risk-management messaging is being refreshed.
- The Q1 2026 Form 10-Q reports total assets of $14.999 billion at March 31, 2026 and 62 branch locations across Long Island, NYC boroughs, Westchester County and New Jersey. Implication: Scale fits Zurich middle-market FI appetite; branch count creates property, premises liability, employee-safety, crime/security and catastrophe-aggregation diligence needs.
- The NYDFS CRA evaluation describes Dime as a New York State-chartered commercial bank offering commercial and consumer banking, merchant card processing, cash management, lockbox, online banking, remote deposit capture, safe-deposit boxes and letters of credit. Implication: Supports Cyber, Financial Lines and operational-risk entry points beyond a basic branch-property program.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- The branch network is concentrated in Greater Long Island, NYC boroughs, Westchester, Staten Island and New Jersey, creating potential coastal wind/flood, urban property, water-damage and crime aggregation questions. Implication: Before quoting Property/GL/Umbrella, run location-level CAT, flood, crime, construction, sprinkler and water-mitigation screening.
- NYDFS reported 59 banking offices and 71 ATMs, including 11 off-site cash-dispensing-only ATMs, indicating cash-handling and premises-security exposures. Implication: Validate robbery controls, alarm monitoring, camera coverage, armored-car contracts, ATM service vendors and guard practices.
- The Q1 2026 filing discloses substantial non-owner-occupied commercial real estate and multifamily/mixed-use loan concentrations and describes heightened risk-management practices for those portfolios. Implication: Financial Lines underwriters should review credit concentration, stress testing, board oversight, D&O/E&O implications and regulatory sensitivity.
- NY metro/Long Island/NJ geographic concentration requiring CAT, flood, wind, water-damage and crime screening.
- Branch and ATM network creates premises, cash-handling, robbery/security, employee-safety and third-party service-provider exposure.
- CRE and multifamily loan concentrations plus rising non-performing assets create Financial Lines and risk-governance scrutiny.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Current broker, incumbent carriers, renewal date, prior Zurich submissions and relationship ownership were not found in public sources. Implication: Zurich should validate internally and through distribution before any outward sales motion.
- Public sources did not provide branch-level construction, sprinkler, alarm, camera, water-shutoff, roof age, flood-zone, or catastrophe modeling details. Implication: Property appetite depends on location schedule and risk-engineering review.
- Business-continuity, disaster-recovery, cyber incident-response testing, vendor risk-transfer standards, and COI/indemnity protocols were not publicly detailed. Implication: Ask CRO/COO/procurement for governance evidence before positioning Cyber, GL, Tech/Cyber liability or vendor-driven risk services.
- Fleet, hired/non-owned auto, mobile banking, repossession activity, armored-car arrangements and foreclosed-property exposure are not clear from public evidence. Implication: Auto, WC, GL and Umbrella appetite require confirmation that these exposures are limited or contractually transferred.

Priority buyers and paths:
- Avinash Reddy, Senior Executive Vice President, Chief Operating Officer and Chief Financial Officer (priority 1, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Lead with a concise rebrand-and-branch-footprint risk review for his combined CFO/COO remit: help validate broker strategy, total cost of risk, location-level CAT/flood/water/crime issues, WC/GL/Umbrella exposures, and Financial Lines/Cyber governance before the next renewal or market check.
  - Business paths: Dime investor relations/management page lists Avi Reddy as the IR contact and publishes a corporate business email path: avinash.reddy@dime.com.; LinkedIn profile path for business outreach: https://www.linkedin.com/in/avinash-reddy-3377ab14.; Corporate email convention is directly supported by the public IR listing as first.last@dime.com; use only for professional outreach after Zurich/broker ownership validation.
  - Warm paths: University of Rochester alumni network based on public education listing.; Former investment banking network from Evercore Partners, Barclays Capital and Lehman Brothers roles disclosed in Dime proxy materials.; Greater Long Island and Metro New York commercial banking ecosystem, where Dime has a visible community-bank and deposit-market position.
- Christopher J. Porzelt, Executive Vice President and Chief Risk Officer (priority 2, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Frame outreach around Dime's June 2026 rebrand and branch footprint as a timely moment to pressure-test enterprise risk governance across property aggregation, cyber resilience, vendor controls, claims analytics and Financial Lines exposures, with Zurich positioned first as a risk-engineering and underwriting-validation partner rather than an immediate quote push.

  - Outreach angle: Approach through Zurich distribution with a post-rebrand operational-resilience angle: Dime's technology and operations leadership is scaling digital banking and commercial core capabilities across a NY-metro branch footprint, so Zurich can offer a broker-led discussion on cyber incident readiness, BCP/DR, vendor-risk transfer, branch/ATM operational controls, and claims scenarios before renewal rather than leading with an immediate quote request.

  - Outreach angle: Frame outreach around helping Dime's legal and governance team pressure-test risk-transfer, claims defensibility, privacy/cyber governance, vendor indemnity, and D&O/E&O coordination after the June 2026 Dime Commercial Bank rebrand and branch-footprint expansion. The ask should be a broker-led validation conversation on renewal timing, current limits, contractual risk-transfer standards, and governance priorities, not an immediate quote request.
  - Business paths: Company-routed contact through Dime's public Contact Us/Help Center and Contact Us Online paths; avoid personal contact data.; Corporate governance or investor-relations routing via Dime's public management/investor-relations pages if a formal business inquiry needs internal forwarding.; Corporate email pattern may be inferable from public Dime executive contact convention as first.last@dime.com, but validate through approved broker or relationship-owner channels before using and do not rely on an inferred individual address.

  - Outreach angle: Frame the discussion around the CHRO remit: after Dime's 2026 rebrand and continued branch footprint across NY metro and Long Island, Zurich can help benchmark Workers Compensation loss drivers, return-to-work practices, branch employee safety, workplace violence readiness and claims analytics while coordinating with risk and operations on broader property and security controls.
  - Business paths: LinkedIn profile: https://www.linkedin.com/in/austinstonitsch; Official Dime management page verifies role and can support executive routing: https://investors.dime.com/corporate-governance/management; Dime public contact/help center and online contact forms: https://www.dime.com/contact-us

Next actions:
- Validate Zurich internal account history, broker relationship and renewal timing immediately; keep the account at watch if no broker path, renewal event, or white-space angle can be confirmed.
- Use the June 2026 rebrand and NYSE transfer/opening-bell event as the business reason to ask CFO/COO/risk leadership about refreshed enterprise risk, cyber and branch-property programs.
- Route any first conversation through distribution around Property/GL/WC/Umbrella plus Financial Lines/Cyber; position risk engineering and claims analytics rather than a monoline quote.
- Run a location-level CAT/crime/flood/water-damage screen on the 62-branch footprint before prioritizing Property capacity.
- Ask for vendor risk-transfer, ATM/cash-handling, BCP/DR and cyber-control artifacts early; move to watch if broker/renewal white space or controls cannot be validated.

Open underwriting questions:
- Is Dime already insured, quoted, declined, or relationship-owned by Zurich North America, and through which broker?
- What are the current package, property, umbrella, WC, auto, FI bond, D&O/E&O and cyber renewal dates, limits, retentions and incumbent carriers?
- Provide complete location schedule with building values, tenant improvements, construction, occupancy, sprinkler status, roof age, flood/wind/hail scores, alarms, cameras and water-mitigation controls.
- What controls govern ATMs, cash handling, armored-car vendors, branch opening/closing, security guards, workplace violence, and robbery prevention?
- Does Dime own or manage foreclosed real estate, repossessed autos, mobile branches, or material owned/hired/non-owned fleet exposure?
- What are the bank's BCP/DR, cyber incident response, vendor due diligence, COI, indemnity, waiver of subrogation and minimum insurance-limit standards?

Scores: ICP 94 | Zurich appetite 72 | Pursuit priority 66 | Evidence confidence 80
Website: https://www.nexbank.com
Subsegment / SIC: Privately held regional commercial bank and financial holding company with institutional, commercial, mortgage banking, securities and title-related affiliates / 6029 Commercial Banks, NEC; parent also fits 6712 Bank Holding Companies

Why this account:
Keep as pursue, but only for active Zurich internal and distribution validation now. NexBank is a clear FI ICP fit with meaningful scale, recent asset growth, a 2024 mortgage-company acquisition, branch consolidation effective March 31, 2026, and multiple entity/coverage-mapping angles. The score is downgraded because the public record does not identify a broker, renewal date, incumbent carrier, Zurich white space, loss history, or physical/cyber control detail. Sales should validate ownership and broker path before deeper buyer outreach.

Why now:
- Q4 2025 public financial highlights show NexBank Capital assets of about $17.5B, up from about $14.4B in 2024, creating a timely account-size and program-structure review trigger.
- NexBank acquired Equity Now, a New York-based mortgage company, in 2024, adding entity-integration and mortgage-professional-risk questions.
- NexBank announced its Uptown branch would permanently close on March 31, 2026, creating a property, lease, business-continuity and premises-liability update trigger.
- The 2025 CRA public file includes FDIC evaluation material, CRA strategic-plan activity and community comments, indicating active regulatory/community scrutiny that can elevate risk governance conversations.
- Public leadership pages identify senior executives over operations, mortgage banking, deposits, IT, information security, legal and HR, giving Zurich clear buyer-persona paths.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Financial Lines, Cyber, Property, General Liability, Umbrella, Workers Compensation, Claims/Risk Engineering, Auto

Positive evidence:
- NexBank Capital, Inc. is a Dallas financial holding company with about $17.5B in assets; its primary subsidiary NexBank is described as the largest privately held bank in Texas by assets and provides institutional, commercial and mortgage banking services nationwide. Implication: Strong ICP fit and sufficient scale for a multiline FI program, including financial lines, cyber, property/GL/umbrella and risk engineering.
- Q4 2025 highlights show total assets increasing to about $17.47B from about $14.37B in 2024, $9.4B gross loan portfolio, and capital ratios above stated well-capitalized regulatory minimums. Implication: Growth supports a timely coverage adequacy, limits, retentions and program-structure conversation with CFO/risk leadership.
- Commercial banking offerings include commercial lending, commercial real estate lending, financial-institution credit lines, holding-company term loans and treasury management. Implication: Creates financial-institutions E&O/D&O, cyber, crime/fidelity, GL/umbrella and contractual-risk-transfer discussion angles.
- FDIC CRA material states NexBank converted to a commercial bank in 2020, is wholly owned by NexBank Capital, and added entities including Government Loan Holding Trust, Public Welfare Investments HoldCo and the 2024 Equity Now mortgage-company acquisition. Implication: Entity schedule and acquisition integration are useful entry points for broker validation and multiline coverage mapping.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- No public broker, carrier, renewal-cycle or Zurich-incumbency evidence is provided in the reviewed profile. Implication: This is the main quality-gate weakness; do not assume white space, and make internal Zurich/broker validation the first action.
- Dallas-only branch concentration creates probable Texas severe-convective-storm, hail and wind property-catastrophe exposure despite limited physical footprint. Implication: Property entry should start with CAT modeling, roof/construction details, water mitigation and leasehold responsibilities.
- CRA public material includes community comments requesting FDIC scrutiny or downgrade, while the 2025 evaluation still reports Satisfactory strategic-plan performance and Low Satisfactory elements in service/lending tests. Implication: Regulatory/community-relations sensitivity may matter for D&O, EPL, reputational-risk and governance conversations.
- Dallas/Texas property concentration with likely hail, wind and severe-convective-storm exposure.
- Mortgage, CRE, warehouse/correspondent lending and acquired mortgage-company complexity.
- Public CRA/community-comment scrutiny and regulatory sensitivity.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Current P&C, financial-lines, cyber and bond brokers/carriers, renewal dates, limits, retentions and Zurich relationship status are unknown. Implication: Validate in Zurich systems and with distribution before outreach.
- Physical risk controls are not publicly verified: owned versus leased premises, construction, roof age, sprinklers, water shutoff, alarms, cameras, guards and branch cash controls. Implication: Needed before judging property/GL appetite beyond initial fit.
- Business-continuity, disaster-recovery, cyber incident-response, vendor-risk-transfer and mortgage-wire controls are not publicly documented in sufficient detail. Implication: Key underwriting discovery for cyber, financial institutions E&O, crime and operational resilience.
- Fleet, hired/non-owned auto, mobile banking, cash transport, self-repossession and foreclosure/repossessed-property exposure are not publicly quantified. Implication: Auto and umbrella appetite depend on whether these exposures are minimal or outsourced with strong risk transfer.

Priority buyers and paths:
- Stacy Hodges, Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, NexBank Capital, Inc. and NexBank (priority 1, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Lead with a CFO-to-CFO/risk-finance framing: NexBank's asset growth, mortgage acquisition integration, entity complexity, and Dallas branch consolidation create a practical moment to validate whether limits, retentions, cyber/financial-lines structure, property CAT treatment, and broker strategy still match the company's current risk profile. The message should acknowledge her finance, accounting, IT, and strategic-planning remit and offer a broker-coordinated review rather than a generic insurance pitch.
  - Business paths: Company route: use NexBank's public contact page and corporate channels first: https://www.nexbank.com/contact.; Broker/distribution route: validate incumbent broker, renewal timing, and Zurich white space through Dallas/Texas financial-institutions brokers before direct buyer outreach.; LinkedIn/business profile route: public LinkedIn profile path surfaced for CFO at NexBank; validate profile ownership before messaging: https://www.linkedin.com/in/stacy-hodges-a18996194.
  - Warm paths: Baylor University accounting/Hankamer alumni network, because NexBank's official bio verifies Hodges earned a BBA in Accounting from Baylor.; Texas Society of CPAs and AICPA finance/accounting networks, because NexBank's official bio verifies membership in both.; KPMG alumni network, because NexBank's official bio says Hodges began her career as a CPA with KPMG LLP.
- Brian Ralston, Executive Vice President and Chief Operating Officer, NexBank Capital, Inc. and NexBank (priority 2, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Lead with a validation-first discussion around Ralston's operational integration remit: NexBank's asset growth, 2024 mortgage-company acquisition context, March 31, 2026 branch consolidation, and his oversight of mortgage banking, deposit/funding strategy, investments, and securities platforms create a practical opening for Zurich to map financial lines, cyber/social-engineering, property/GL/umbrella, BCP, vendor-risk-transfer, and claims/risk-engineering controls before any quote strategy.
  - Business paths: Use NexBank's public contact page and general corporate inquiry form for business-safe routing; do not send confidential underwriting information through the form.; LinkedIn profile path for professional outreach: https://www.linkedin.com/in/brian-ralston-a2178b40.; Corporate location path: NexBank lists its corporate office at 2515 McKinney Avenue, Suite 1100, Dallas, Texas 75201 on its public contact page.
  - Warm paths: University of California, Santa Barbara alumni path, only if the LinkedIn-directory education signal is confirmed before use.; Mortgage banking conference network path, since NexBank's bio says Ralston has spoken or served as a panelist on wholesale, correspondent, and warehouse lending topics.; HousingWire or mortgage-media ecosystem path, given HousingWire coverage of his 2022 mortgage-banking promotion and NexBank's mortgage-lender positioning.
- Michelle Johnson, Executive Vice President and Chief Administrative Officer, NexBank Capital, Inc. and NexBank (priority 3, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: A broker-led note should connect to Johnson's cross-functional remit: NexBank's asset growth, 2024 mortgage-company acquisition, and 2026 branch consolidation create a practical reason to review entity schedules, cyber and business-continuity controls, legal/vendor risk transfer, employee/HR exposures, and property/CAT implications across financial lines, cyber, crime, property, GL, and umbrella before the next renewal cycle.
  - Business paths: Use NexBank's public Contact Us page for general business inquiry routing: https://www.nexbank.com/contact; Use the NexBank leadership bio page as the verified executive profile path: https://www.nexbank.com/about; Use Michelle Johnson's public LinkedIn profile path for professional-context outreach: https://www.linkedin.com/in/michelle-johnson-a0406a48
  - Warm paths: Zurich or broker relationship with NexBank, NexBank Capital, NexBank Securities, NexBank Title, Equity Now, or NexPoint-adjacent entities could create the cleanest introduction path.; Dallas financial-institution broker networks are likely more actionable than cold outreach because public evidence does not identify NexBank's insurance broker or renewal cycle.; Company-level community partners listed by NexBank, including Dallas Area Habitat for Humanity, The Real Estate Council Community Fund, Texas Women's Foundation, Family Gateway, and North Texas Food Bank, may create Dallas civic-network paths; no personal Johnson affiliation was verified.
- Rhett Miller, Executive Vice President and Chief Banking Officer, NexBank Capital, Inc. and NexBank (priority 4, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Lead with a validation-first conversation around NexBank's growth, commercial and institutional banking expansion, CRE and credit governance, the 2024 mortgage-company integration and the March 31, 2026 branch consolidation. Position Zurich as helping pressure-test entity schedules, financial-lines/cyber/crime controls, CAT-exposed Dallas property assumptions and operational-risk evidence before any quote strategy, rather than as a generic bank-insurance pitch.
  - Business paths: Use NexBank's public contact page and corporate channels to route any business inquiry: https://www.nexbank.com/contact.; Use the public LinkedIn profile path for business-contextual outreach or profile validation: https://www.linkedin.com/in/rhett-miller-82924518.; Prefer a broker or Zurich distribution introduction after internal account lookup; no public broker, incumbent carrier or renewal path was verified.
  - Warm paths: University of Dallas MBA alumni network in Dallas; validate current alumni access before use.; Florida State University finance/alumni network; validate relevance before use.; Former Southwest Securities, FSB and MMA Realty Capital colleagues or Dallas CRE finance contacts may be practical business introductions because those roles are listed in public bio sources.
- Matt Siekielski, Director; President and Chief Executive Officer, NexBank Capital, Inc. and NexBank (priority 5, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Approach only after Zurich internal and broker validation, positioning the conversation as a CEO-level risk governance review around NexBank's asset growth, mortgage-company integration, Dallas branch/property changes, and entity schedule complexity, with Zurich potentially helping benchmark multiline financial-institution coverage, cyber/wire-fraud controls, financial lines, CAT property, GL/umbrella, and claims/risk-engineering coordination.
  - Business paths: Company contact/contact-us route through NexBank website and Dallas corporate office; use for general business routing only, not as a direct executive email path: https://www.nexbank.com/contact; LinkedIn business profile path for professional context: https://www.linkedin.com/in/matt-siekielski-a460b020a; Broker/distribution intro through Dallas or Texas financial-institution brokers after Zurich account lookup; incumbent broker and renewal month are not public.
  - Warm paths: University of Texas at Arlington alumni network, tied to his MBA in Accounting and MS in Real Estate.; Southwestern University alumni network, tied to his BA in Business Administration.; The Real Estate Council Community Fund and Dallas commercial real estate/community development finance network, based on official bio board-service disclosure.

Next actions:
- Run Zurich internal account lookup for NexBank, NexBank Capital and known affiliates before external outreach.
- Ask Dallas/Texas FI brokers for incumbent carrier, renewal month, decision maker and appetite friction; treat the first step as distribution validation.
- Position the first conversation around asset growth, mortgage acquisition integration and branch consolidation rather than generic bank insurance.
- Target CFO, COO, Chief Administrative Officer, General Counsel/CRO and procurement/risk contacts through public business channels or broker introduction only.
- Request entity schedule, current program summary, property schedule, cyber/BCP controls, vendor-risk-transfer standards and loss runs before proposing lines.

Open underwriting questions:
- Is NexBank, NexBank Capital or any affiliate already insured, quoted or relationship-owned by Zurich, and through which broker?
- What is the full legal-entity schedule, including NexBank Capital, NexBank, NexBank Securities, NexBank Title, Equity Now, trusts and investment subsidiaries?
- What are the property schedule, lease obligations, construction, roof age, sprinkler/alarm/camera protections, water mitigation and CAT modeling results for Dallas locations?
- How is the March 31, 2026 Uptown branch closure being handled for premises liability, records, employees, vendors, clients and business continuity?
- What controls govern mortgage wire transfer, social engineering, title/closing errors, vendor access, data security, cyber incident response and disaster recovery?
- Are cash transport, guards, repossessions, foreclosed properties, mobile banking or fleet/HNOA exposures present, and are they outsourced with certificates, indemnity and waivers?

Source coverage: Company website/about and leadership, Company contact/locations notice, Company commercial-banking service pages, FDIC CRA public file and official regulatory material, SEC broker-dealer audit filing, FDIC BankFind reference
Key sources: https://www.nexbank.com, https://www.nexbank.com/about, https://www.nexbank.com/contact, https://www.nexbank.com/service/commercial-banking, https://www.nexbank.com/admin/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/2026-CRA-Public-File-FINAL.pdf, https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1306765/000130676525000001/nexbank.2024audit1.pdf, https://banks.data.fdic.gov/bankfind-suite/bankfind/details/29209

### Centennial Bank

Recommendation: watch
Scores: ICP 95 | Zurich appetite 69 | Pursuit priority 64 | Evidence confidence 82
Website: https://www.my100bank.com/
Subsegment / SIC: FDIC-insured regional commercial bank and Home BancShares subsidiary with multi-state branch banking, commercial lending, mortgage, wealth, treasury, dealer finance, marine finance, and affiliated insurance-agency activity. / 6022 State Banks, Federal Reserve

Why this account:
Centennial Bank is a strong Zurich Financial Institutions ICP fit and still deserves buyer research: it is an FDIC-insured regional bank with material assets, 242 offices, public-company governance, multi-state branch/property exposure, recent acquisition integration, and credible multiline needs. However, the original pursue call is slightly overstated under the calibration standard because public evidence does not identify broker, renewal timing, incumbent carriers, Zurich white space, loss history, or property-control quality. Treat as watch with buyer research approved: validate internal Zurich status and distribution path before active external pursuit.

Why now:
- Home BancShares completed the Mountain Commerce Bancorp acquisition on April 1, 2026, merging Mountain Commerce Bank into Centennial and adding Tennessee operations, which creates an insurance-program integration trigger.
- FDIC data cited in the profile shows Centennial at 242 offices and about $23.0B in assets, indicating enough scale for multiline regional-bank buyer research.
- Home BancShares reported record total loans of $15.69B at December 31, 2025 and $400.2M quarterly loan growth, increasing operational and financial-lines exposure.
- Public investor and acquisition materials flag acquisition integration, cyber/fraud, hurricanes/adverse weather, and natural-disaster risks, supporting timely underwriting discovery if a broker or internal owner confirms access.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Property, General Liability, Workers Compensation, Umbrella, Financial Lines, Cyber, Claims/Risk Engineering

Positive evidence:
- Centennial Bank is an active FDIC-insured institution headquartered in Conway, Arkansas, with FDIC certificate 11241, about $23.0B in assets, and 242 offices in the cited FDIC data. Implication: Strong ICP fit and enough operating scale to justify Zurich internal account matching and buyer research.
- FDIC branch data shows a broad office footprint across Arkansas, Florida, Texas, Tennessee, Alabama, and New York. Implication: Creates credible Property, GL, WC, Umbrella, and claims analytics discovery around branch schedules, catastrophe exposure, premises controls, and loss trends.
- Home BancShares reported $22.88B in assets, $17.48B in deposits, record loans of $15.69B, and non-performing assets of 0.55% at December 31, 2025. Implication: Financial scale and performance support buyer research for a risk-improvement and multiline placement conversation.
- The April 1, 2026 Mountain Commerce acquisition merged Mountain Commerce Bank into Centennial and added Tennessee branches and integration work. Implication: M&A integration is the clearest near-term trigger for broker outreach, exposure review, and renewal-cycle discovery.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- The profile does not provide public evidence of current broker, incumbent carrier tower, renewal date, Zurich relationship status, or prior Zurich quote history. Implication: This is the main reason to downgrade from pursue to watch; validate distribution path and white space before external sales pursuit.
- Branch concentrations include Florida, Texas, Alabama, Tennessee, Arkansas, and New York, creating wind, hail, flood, hurricane, convective-storm, and urban-premises scrutiny even though exact TIV and controls are not public. Implication: Do not assume property appetite; lead buyer research with schedule quality, catastrophe modeling, and property-control discovery.
- Home BancShares reported $39.8M of foreclosed assets held for sale at December 31, 2025, including Texas and Centennial CFG exposure. Implication: Zurich should request REO schedules, property-condition controls, and third-party management/risk-transfer protocols before appetite confirmation.
- Large multi-state branch property schedule with meaningful Florida, Texas, Alabama, Tennessee, Arkansas, and New York exposure.
- Recent Mountain Commerce acquisition creates integration, systems, personnel, branch, vendor, and insurance-consolidation uncertainty.
- Publicly reported foreclosed assets and non-performing assets require REO and collateral-property review.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Current corporate P&C broker, carrier tower, renewal date, Zurich incumbent status, and prior Zurich quote history are not publicly verified. Implication: Run Zurich CRM, policy, quote, and broker-distribution checks before external pursuit.
- Branch-level property controls are not public: owned vs leased locations, construction, roof age, sprinklers, water mitigation, alarms, cameras, flood zones, wind/hail deductibles, and TIV by location. Implication: Required before Property appetite and catastrophe pricing can be assessed.
- Fleet, hired/non-owned auto, mobile banking, ATM servicing, cash transport, repossession, and employee driving exposure are not public. Implication: Auto should remain secondary unless operations discovery shows controlled fleet or meaningful HNOA exposure.
- Workers compensation exposure, headcount by state, call-center concentration, branch staffing, security-guard model, and workplace-violence controls are not public. Implication: Validate WC appetite and loss-control needs through broker submission or buyer discovery.

Priority buyers and paths:
- Brian S. Davis, Chief Financial Officer, Treasurer and Director of Home BancShares and Centennial Bank (priority 1, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: A broker-led note to Davis should start with finance and integration, not product: Home BancShares' 2026 Mountain Commerce integration and multi-state Centennial branch footprint create a timely reason to benchmark total cost of risk, branch/TIV and REO data quality, cyber/BCP controls, and limits/deductible structure across property, casualty, cyber, and financial-lines towers before any external market approach.
  - Business paths: Use Home BancShares Investor Relations as the cleanest public company route; the company site lists Donna Townsell as Director of Investor Relations and provides public IR contact information.; Use the official Home BancShares officers/directors page and SEC proxy as identity-validation links before any broker or CRM matching.; Use LinkedIn as a business-profile path only; do not rely on login-only details or unverified personal contact data.
  - Warm paths: University of Arkansas at Fayetteville alumni network, because the SEC proxy identifies Davis as a graduate.; Arkansas banking-finance network through his verified prior roles at Simmons First National Corporation and Worthen Banking Corporation.; Public-company investor-relations ecosystem through Home BancShares earnings calls and covering analysts/corporate-access channels.
- Stephen Tipton, Chief Executive Officer, Centennial Bank (Home BancShares subsidiary) (priority 2, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: Position Zurich as a risk-improvement and integration partner around Centennial's post-acquisition operating footprint: branch property schedule quality, catastrophe-exposed locations, REO/collateral-property controls, BCP/cyber resilience, and claims analytics. For Tipton, the relevant hook is his CEO role plus documented operations, credit-risk, lending, and acquisition background; ask for the appropriate risk, finance, facilities, or broker owner after Zurich confirms distribution access and renewal timing.
  - Business paths: Use the Home BancShares public Information Request Form for routed corporate contact: https://www.homebancshares.com/more-information/information-request-form/default.aspx; Use Home BancShares/Centennial executive and investor-relations pages as business-safe routing points before any executive outreach: https://www.homebancshares.com/overview/officers-directors/default.aspx; LinkedIn path: search Stephen Tipton with Centennial Bank/Home BancShares; no direct individual LinkedIn profile URL was verified quickly from public sources, so do not rely on a guessed profile.
  - Warm paths: University of Arkansas alumni network is a credible warm-intro route because public bios verify Tipton as a University of Arkansas at Fayetteville graduate.; Arkansas banking and business-media ecosystem is plausible: Tipton was profiled by Arkansas Money & Politics and covered in regional banking/business publications around the CEO appointment.; Home BancShares acquisition network is relevant because Tipton's public bio includes acquisition experience and Centennial recently integrated Mountain Commerce Bank into its footprint.
- Kyle Penter, CRCM, Chief Risk Officer, Centennial Bank (priority 3, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: Frame outreach around helping Centennial validate risk-transfer and control evidence after the Mountain Commerce integration: Zurich can support a structured risk review across cyber/BCP, third-party risk, branch property/cat exposure, REO/collateral controls, umbrella/liability, and claims analytics without assuming property appetite until schedules, controls, loss runs, and broker access are confirmed.
  - Business paths: LinkedIn business profile path: https://www.linkedin.com/in/kyle-penter-crcm-262406143.; Centennial Bank general public contact path: https://www.my100bank.com/contact-us/; route through corporate/risk-management channels rather than using personal contact data.; Home BancShares/Centennial public corporate channel via annual-report headquarters and investor-relations routing; ask for appropriate enterprise risk or insurance-risk contact.
  - Warm paths: Arkansas State University alumni network, based on the public LinkedIn search result; verify before using.; Conway and Central Arkansas banking/business community, supported by the 501 LIFE business profile and Centennial/Home BancShares headquarters presence.; Centennial/Home BancShares executive network, especially risk, compliance, information security, investor relations, facilities, and legal contacts tied to enterprise risk oversight.
- Kevin W. Bartholomew, Chief Information Officer, Centennial Bank (priority 4, confidence medium)
  - Outreach angle: Position Zurich as a cyber and resilience risk-engineering resource for a multi-state regional bank integrating new operations: offer a short, insurance-ready controls discussion around incident response, BCP/DR evidence, third-party/vendor risk, branch disruption scenarios, and how Centennial can present its controls cleanly for cyber and financial-lines underwriting without assuming a current placement opportunity.
  - Business paths: Centennial Bank public website and corporate contact/branch channels via https://www.my100bank.com/; route professionally to IT, cyber risk, business continuity, or the CIO office rather than using personal channels.; LinkedIn professional profile path for business-safe outreach: https://www.linkedin.com/in/kevin-bartholomew-69209020.; Home BancShares corporate/investor-relations channels in the SEC annual report can be used for executive-level routing if Zurich has a legitimate business purpose.
  - Warm paths: Mutual LinkedIn connections in Conway, Arkansas banking, technology, and risk circles may be useful because the public LinkedIn result lists Conway and 500+ connections.; Home BancShares/Centennial executive-staff network named in the annual report, including CEO/COO/CFO and investor-relations leadership, may provide a warm internal route if Zurich already has relationships at the parent company.; Business continuity and disaster-response ecosystem is relevant: Centennial's 2020 CSR report quotes Bartholomew on pandemic, civil unrest, hurricane, tornado, and business-continuity software response work.
- Kevin D. Hester, President & Chief Lending Officer, Home BancShares, Inc. and Centennial Bank; board member of Centennial Bank (priority 5, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Approach Hester through an approved broker or corporate channel with a narrow, underwriting-safe message: as the long-tenured senior lender now leading Home BancShares and Centennial, he can help frame how the Mountain Commerce integration, loan growth, CRE/C&I collateral, REO/property preservation, and cyber/fraud controls intersect with Centennial's insurance program. Zurich's value proposition should be an exposure-quality review across property-cat, financial lines, cyber, umbrella, and risk engineering, not an assumption that he owns the placement.
  - Business paths: Route through Home BancShares Investor Relations / official Contact navigation on homebancshares.com; public release identifies Donna Townsell as Director of Investor Relations, so use corporate IR routing rather than personal contact details.; Use Centennial Bank public website corporate/contact or branch-routing channels at my100bank.com only for proper business routing, not consumer-support outreach.; LinkedIn public profile path can support a business-network introduction or mutual-connection request: https://www.linkedin.com/in/kevin-hester-6a052639.
  - Warm paths: University of Central Arkansas alumni and accounting network may be a credible warm path if Zurich, broker, or client contacts share that affiliation.; National Commercial Lending School / senior commercial-lending network may provide peer introductions relevant to lending risk and collateral controls.; Arkansas banking and Conway/Little Rock business ecosystem, including Home BancShares and Centennial executive or board relationships, is likely the strongest local business intro path.

Next actions:
- Run Zurich internal account matching for Home BancShares, Centennial Bank, Happy State Bank, Mountain Commerce Bank, Centennial Insurance Agency, and Cook Insurance Agency before external outreach.
- Ask distribution partners for broker, renewal, incumbent-carrier, and white-space intelligence tied to the April 2026 Mountain Commerce integration and 242-office branch schedule.
- Approve buyer research focused on CFO/Treasurer, COO, General Counsel/CRO, risk/procurement, facilities/real estate, and insurance-agency relationship mapping.
- Request branch schedule, TIV, REO schedule, loss runs, fleet/HNOA details, vendor-risk-transfer standards, and cyber/BCP control summaries before appetite commitment.
- Model Zurich appetite separately for property-cat-exposed locations versus Financial Lines, Cyber, Umbrella, and Claims/Risk Engineering opportunities so a difficult property layer does not block other entry lines.

Open underwriting questions:
- What is the current insurance tower by line, broker, incumbent carriers, renewal date, deductibles, limits, loss runs, and Zurich prior-quote/account status?
- Which Centennial and newly acquired Mountain Commerce properties are owned vs leased, and what are TIV, construction, roof age, sprinkler, alarm, camera, water mitigation, and catastrophe-zone details by location?
- How are foreclosed assets, repossessed collateral, marine/dealer finance collateral, inspections, property preservation, and repossessions managed, and what vendor risk-transfer terms apply?
- Does Centennial operate owned vehicles, mobile branches, cash transport, ATM service vehicles, or regular employee driving, and what fleet/HNOA controls exist?
- What are the bank's cyber, BCP/DR, vendor-risk, MFA, incident-response, ransomware, and social-engineering controls, and has it had reportable cyber/fraud losses?
- How will Mountain Commerce branches, employees, vendors, systems, and insurance programs be integrated into Centennial in 2026?

Source coverage: Company website, FDIC regulator institution data, FDIC branch-location data, SEC annual report, Public company investor release, Acquisition press release
Key sources: https://www.my100bank.com/, https://api.fdic.gov/banks/institutions?filters=CERT:11241&fields=NAME,CERT,CITY,STNAME,ACTIVE,ASSET,OFFICES,SPECGRP,SPECGRPDESC,CHARTER,REGAGNT,ESTYMD,WEBADDR&format=json, https://api.fdic.gov/banks/locations?filters=CERT:11241&fields=NAME,CERT,CITY,STNAME,ADDRESS,OFFNAME,SERVTYPE&limit=10000&format=json, https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1331520/000133152026000074/a2025annualreport.htm, https://www.homebancshares.com/news-events/news/news-details/2026/HOMB-Delivers-Another-Strong-Quarter-400-Million-Loan-Growth-Sub-40-Efficiency-Ratio--Robust-Margin-Drive-18-Year-Over-Year-Income-Increase-and-Annual-ROA-of-2-10/default.aspx, https://www.mcb.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/Home-BancShares-Inc-Announces-Completion-of-the-Acquisition-of-Mountain-Commerce-Bancorp-Inc-04012026.pdf

### Columbia Bank

Recommendation: watch
Scores: ICP 96 | Zurich appetite 74 | Pursuit priority 64 | Evidence confidence 82
Website: https://www.columbiabankonline.com
Subsegment / SIC: Federally chartered savings/community bank and Columbia Financial operating bank, with commercial and consumer banking, title insurance, insurance-agency, and wealth-management-related services. / 6035 Federal Savings Institutions

Why this account:
Columbia Bank is a strong ICP-fit financial institution with credible multiline insurance need and a concrete 2026 M&A/conversion trigger. However, the profile does not verify broker, incumbent carrier, renewal timing, Zurich relationship status, or true white-space evidence, so a skeptical quality gate should not classify it as active pursue yet. Approve for buyer research and Zurich internal/broker validation, but hold at watch until relationship ownership and renewal opportunity are confirmed.

Why now:
- Federal Reserve approval on May 8, 2026 permits Columbia Financial to become a new top-tier savings and loan holding company and acquire Northfield Bancorp/Northfield Bank, creating a potential consolidation and renewal-review trigger.
- The 2025 annual report describes a second-step conversion plan and Northfield merger agreement, both of which can affect governance, D&O, cyber, crime, property, and umbrella buying needs.
- Columbia opened new branches in Paterson, Rutherford, and Clifton in 2025 and continued renovations across the branch network, creating updated property/GL/WC risk-engineering questions.
- Columbia acquired assets of Jeanne S. Frey Insurance Agency in 2025, expanding insurance-channel activity while making broker path validation more important.

Likely Zurich entry LOBs: Property, General Liability, Workers Compensation, Umbrella, Financial Lines, Cyber, Claims/Risk Engineering

Positive evidence:
- FDIC official data lists Columbia Bank as an active Fair Lawn, New Jersey institution, FDIC certificate 28834, with about $11.009B in assets and 69 offices. Implication: Strong ICP fit for Zurich FI middle-market/regional-bank appetite; validate whether Zurich already writes or has quoted the account.
- The 2025 annual report describes Columbia Bank as a federal savings bank/covered savings association offering deposits, commercial real estate loans, commercial business loans, residential loans, consumer loans, title insurance, insurance agency services, and third-party wealth management services. Implication: Supports a multiline FI conversation across property/casualty, financial lines, cyber, and vendor/third-party controls.
- Company sources show a broad physical footprint: the annual report says 71 full-service banking offices across 12 New Jersey counties, while the branch page lists corporate headquarters and many NJ branches. Implication: Property, GL, WC, umbrella, premises security, water mitigation, and branch COPE review are credible entry points.
- The annual report says Columbia owns 33 properties and leases 38 properties, with First Jersey Title Services and Columbia Insurance Services operating inside branch facilities. Implication: Zurich can test owned/leased property schedule quality, lease risk transfer, additional insured/waiver provisions, and branch maintenance controls.

Negative evidence / risk flags:
- No public broker, incumbent carrier, renewal date, or Zurich relationship evidence was verified in the researched sources. Implication: This is the main reason to downgrade from pursue to watch; Zurich should check CRM/submissions and broker channels before outreach.
- The branch footprint is concentrated in New Jersey and the New York/Philadelphia-adjacent market, so flood, storm, urban-premises, and regional aggregation exposures need screening. Implication: Run location-level cat, flood, crime, and COPE screens before positioning property capacity.
- The Northfield merger and second-step conversion carry execution, regulatory, employee/customer retention, vendor-contract, and integration risks disclosed in company materials. Implication: Use as a why-now trigger, but expect decision timing and incumbent-broker control to be more complex during closing/integration.
- Large NJ branch concentration with flood/storm/crime aggregation to screen by address.
- M&A/conversion integration risk and possible near-term decision-maker distraction.
- Unknown broker, incumbent carrier, renewal timing, and Zurich relationship status.

Unknowns Zurich must validate:
- Current corporate broker, carrier panel, policy renewal dates, program structure, retentions, loss-sensitive features, and Zurich account history are unknown. Implication: Mandatory Zurich internal and broker-channel validation before classifying as true white space.
- Columbia/Northfield combined post-close branch schedule, branch closures, new leases, owned-property values, and construction/renovation details are not fully public. Implication: Needed for property TIV, COPE, cat aggregation, and lease risk-transfer review.
- Fleet size, hired/non-owned auto, mobile banking, courier/cash transport, repossession practices, and armed-guard use were not verified publicly. Implication: Resolve before adding auto or security-sensitive GL/umbrella assumptions.
- Public sources did not provide loss runs or claims history for water damage, robbery, slip/fall, WC, EPL, cyber incidents, regulatory claims, or D&O litigation. Implication: Claims history is required to calibrate appetite and price adequacy.

Priority buyers and paths:
- Thomas F. Splaine, Jr., Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer of Columbia Financial, Inc. and Columbia Bank; designated Principal Financial Officer and Principal Accounting Officer for SEC reporting (priority 1, confidence high)
  - Outreach angle: Lead with a finance-led insurance program validation tied to the Columbia/Northfield transaction: offer Zurich's help benchmarking combined program structure, retentions, claims analytics, cyber integration, D&O continuity, property schedule harmonization, and branch risk-engineering needs before renewal decisions are locked. Position the ask as a brief validation conversation on timing, broker ownership, and post-close priorities, not as a carrier quote request until internal and broker-channel checks confirm white space.
  - Business paths: Official Columbia Bank Contact Us page for routing to corporate or investor relations: https://www.columbiabankonline.com/contact-us; Columbia Financial investor relations site path, including Investor Contacts and Information Request navigation: https://ir.columbiabankonline.com/corporate-leadership/default.aspx; Public LinkedIn profile path located: https://www.linkedin.com/in/thomas-splaine-51105137; use professional messaging only and treat company/SEC sources as authoritative for current title.
